title: Understanding Maudū al-Mudāf ʻalayh and Possessive Constructions in Arabic Grammar
summary: This session focuses on the grammatical structure of maḍūf and maḍūf ʻalayh (possessive constructions) in Modern Standard Arabic. The teacher explains how these units function together, emphasizing pronunciation rules for definite and indefinite nouns, as well as the role of harfu al-jar (prepositions that cause kasrah). Key examples include 'kitāb al-Ustādhi' (the teacher's book) and the use of ya in addressing others. The class also touches upon practical applications in reading and speaking Arabic.
key_points: The maḍūf (e.g., كِتَاب) does not take an alif-lām or tanwīn, while the maḍūf ʻalayh (e.g., حَامِد in بَيْتُ حَامِדٍ) must be in the kasrah to indicate possession.
Definiteness depends on the maḍūf ʻalayh: if it is definite (with alif-lām or proper noun), the maḍūf becomes definite; otherwise, it remains indefinite.
Harfu al-jar (e.g., fī, bi-) cause the following word to take a kasrah. For example, 'fi kitāb Allāhi' (in God's book).
Ya (يا) used for addressing someone triggers a dhamma on the following noun or name. Example: 'ya Ḥamdu-lāh' (Oh Hamid).
questions: [00:15:03] Q: Which surah mentions 'fi kitabillahi'? — A: Surah Ahzab, but the exact verse was not specified by the teacher.

quiz.q: What is the term for the first word in a possessive construction (e.g., كِتَابُ in كِتَابُ حَامِدٍ)?
options: A. مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ
B. مُضَافٌ
C. حَرْفُ الْجَرِّ
D. مُبْتَدَأٌ
quiz.a: The first word in an iḍāfa construction is the مُضَافٌ. It neither takes tanwīn nor the definite article اَلْ.

quiz.q: In the phrase كِتَابُ رَجُلٍ, is the construction definite or indefinite — and why?
options: A. Definite, because كِتَابُ is the first word
B. Indefinite, because رَجُلٍ is an indefinite noun
C. Definite, because all iḍāfa constructions are definite
D. Indefinite, because the مُضَافٌ has no haraka
quiz.a: Definiteness is determined by the مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ. Since رَجُلٍ is an indefinite common noun (with tanwīn), the whole construction كِتَابُ رَجُلٍ is indefinite — meaning 'a book of a man.'

quiz.q: In the phrase كِتَابُ حَامِدٍ, why is the construction considered definite even though حَامِدٍ carries tanwīn?
options: A. Because tanwīn always indicates definiteness
B. Because حَامِدٌ is a proper noun and proper nouns are inherently definite
C. Because the مُضَافٌ has a ḍamma
D. Because iḍāfa constructions are always definite
quiz.a: Proper nouns refer to specific individuals and are therefore definite regardless of tanwīn. حَامِدٌ names a specific person, so كِتَابُ حَامِدٍ means 'Hamid's book' (definite).

quiz.q: What haraka does the مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ always take (in the general rule)?
options: A. Fatḥa (زَبَر)
B. Ḍamma (پيش)
C. Kasra (زير)
D. Sukūn
quiz.a: The مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ is always in the genitive case and therefore takes a kasra (or double kasra for indefinite nouns) at the end.

quiz.q: What are the two things the مُضَافٌ (first word in an iḍāfa) must NOT have?
options: A. A kasra and a fatḥa
B. Tanwīn and the definite article اَلْ
C. A ḍamma and a sukūn
D. A shadda and a madda
quiz.a: The defining feature of the مُضَافٌ is that it is stripped of both tanwīn and اَلْ. This signals to the reader that a possessive construction has begun.

quiz.q: What grammatical term is given to يَا when it is used to address or call someone (e.g., يَا يَاسِرُ)?
options: A. حَرْفُ الْجَرِّ
B. حَرْفُ النِّدَاءِ
C. حَرْفُ الْعَطْفِ
D. أَدَاةُ الِاسْتِفْهَامِ
quiz.a: يَا belongs to the family of حَرْفُ النِّدَاءِ — particles of calling/address (nidāʾ). It is used to call out to someone.

quiz.q: When يَا precedes a simple (non-muḍāf) noun or name, what haraka does that noun take?
options: A. Double ḍamma (tanwīn al-rafʿ)
B. Kasra
C. Single ḍamma only
D. Fatḥa
quiz.a: The general rule of nidāʾ: a simple noun after يَا takes a single ḍamma (no tanwīn). So يَاسِرٌ becomes يَا يَاسِرُ.

quiz.q: In the Qur'ānic phrase إِذْ يُبَايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ, why does الشَّجَرَةِ end in a kasra?
options: A. It is the مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ of تَحْتَ
B. It follows a ḥarf al-jarr
C. It is a feminine noun
D. Both A and C
quiz.a: تَحْتَ is a ẓarf (adverb of place) that functions like an iḍāfa: the word following it is treated as its مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ and therefore takes a kasra. الشَّجَرَةِ = 'the tree.'

quiz.q: What is the difference between هُنَا and هُنَاكَ?
options: A. هُنَا means 'there' and هُنَاكَ means 'here'
B. هُنَا means 'here' and هُنَاكَ means 'there'
C. Both mean 'here' in different dialects
D. هُنَا is a verb and هُنَاكَ is a noun
quiz.a: هُنَا means 'here' (proximity) while هُنَاكَ means 'there' (distance). Both are adverbs of place (ẓarf al-makān).

quiz.q: The phrase فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ contains multiple grammatical features covered in this lesson. Which of the following correctly describes it?
options: A. فِي is a ḥarf al-jarr making كِتَابِ take kasra; كِتَابِ اللَّهِ is an iḍāfa making اللَّهِ take kasra
B. فِي is a ḥarf al-jarr making كِتَابِ take fatḥa; اللَّهِ takes ḍamma
C. كِتَابِ takes kasra only because of the iḍāfa; فِي has no effect
D. اللَّهِ takes tanwīn because it follows a ḥarf al-jarr
quiz.a: Two rules apply simultaneously: (1) فِي (ḥarf al-jarr) causes كِتَابِ to take kasra; (2) within the iḍāfa كِتَابِ اللَّهِ, اللَّهِ is the مُضَافٌ إِلَيْهِ and also takes kasra. اللَّهِ never takes tanwīn because it etymologically contains اَلْ.

quran_examples.arabic: وَأُولُو الْأَرْحَامِ بَعْضُهُمْ أَوْلَىٰ بِبَعْضٍ فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ
quran_examples.transliteration: wa-ūlū al-arḥāmi baʿḍuhum awlā bi-baʿḍin fī kitābi llāh
quran_examples.translation: And those of [blood] relationship are more entitled [to inheritance] in the decree of Allāh
quran_examples.note: Demonstrates فِي كِتَابِ اللَّهِ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ: the ḥarf al-jarr فِي makes كِتَابِ take kasra, and كِتَابِ اللَّهِ is itself a muḍāf–muḍāf ilayhi construction where اللَّهِ (which inherently contains اَلْ and therefore never takes tanwīn) takes kasra as the muḍāf ilayh.

quran_examples.arabic: لَّقَدْ رَضِيَ اللَّهُ عَنِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ إِذْ يُبَايِعُونَكَ تَحْتَ الشَّجَرَةِ
quran_examples.transliteration: la-qad raḍiya llāhu ʿani l-muʾminīna idh yubāyiʿūnaka taḥta l-shajarah
quran_examples.translation: Certainly Allāh was pleased with the believers when they pledged allegiance to you under the tree
quran_examples.note: Demonstrates تَحْتَ (taḥta/under) as a ẓarf al-makān: the word following it, الشَّجَرَةِ, takes kasra because it functions as the muḍāf ilayh of تَحْتَ — exactly as taught in this lesson.

quran_examples.arabic: وَإِذْ جَعَلْنَا الْبَيْتَ مَثَابَةً لِّلنَّاسِ وَأَمْنًا
quran_examples.transliteration: wa-idh jaʿalnā l-bayta mathābatan li-n-nāsi wa-amnā
quran_examples.translation: And [recall] when We made the House a place of return for the people and [a place of] security
quran_examples.note: The word الْبَيْتَ here refers to بَيْتُ اللَّهِ عَزَّ وَجَلَّ (the House of Allāh), the Kaʿba — the iḍāfa construction بَيْتُ اللَّهِ was used in class as a prime example of muḍāf + muḍāf ilayh.

quran_examples.translation: In it are clear signs [such as] the standing place of Ibrāhīm (ʿalayhi l-salām)
quran_examples.note: مَقَامُ إِبْرَاهِيمَ is a muḍāf–muḍāf ilayhi construction: مَقَامُ (the standing place) is the muḍāf with no tanwīn or اَلْ, and إِبْرَاهِيمَ is the muḍāf ilayh. As a diptote (ممنوع من الصرف), إِبْرَاهِيمَ takes fatḥa instead of kasra in the genitive — a nuance the teacher acknowledged as an exception to learn later.
